inline_review:
commit 5502094cb825f8d248d81d6f849c5b94a278d6c1
Author: Pasha Tatashin <pasha.tatashin@soleen.com>

liveupdate: Make unregister functions return void

Change liveupdate_unregister_file_handler and liveupdate_unregister_flb
to return void instead of an error code. This follows the design
principle that unregistration during module unload should not fail,
as the unload cannot be stopped at that point.

> diff --git a/include/linux/liveupdate.h b/include/linux/liveupdate.h
> index 8394fb2d8774..636175432d62 100644
> --- a/include/linux/liveupdate.h
> +++ b/include/linux/liveupdate.h

[ ... ]

> @@ -231,12 +231,12 @@ bool liveupdate_enabled(void);
>  int liveupdate_reboot(void);
>  
>  int liveupdate_register_file_handler(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh);
> -int liveupdate_unregister_file_handler(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh);
> +void liveupdate_unregister_file_handler(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh);
>  
>  int liveupdate_register_flb(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh,
>  			    struct liveupdate_flb *flb);

Does liveupdate_register_flb() properly validate zero-initialized handlers?

If a caller erroneously passes a zero-initialized file handler that has not
been passed to liveupdate_register_file_handler(), fh->private.list.next
is NULL.

The list_empty() macro compares head->next == head, which evaluates to false
when NULL == &fh->private.list, bypassing the WARN_ON check.

Since flb_list is also zero-initialized, list_add_tail() will attempt to
dereference NULL (head->prev->next = new), causing a kernel panic.

Should this explicitly verify initialization instead of using list_empty()?

> -int liveupdate_unregister_flb(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh,
> -			      struct liveupdate_flb *flb);
> +void liveupdate_unregister_flb(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh,
> +			       struct liveupdate_flb *flb);

> diff --git a/kernel/liveupdate/luo_file.c b/kernel/liveupdate/luo_file.c
> index c9c26015020b..a38ea4975824 100644
> --- a/kernel/liveupdate/luo_file.c
> +++ b/kernel/liveupdate/luo_file.c
> @@ -874,15 +874,13 @@ int liveupdate_register_file_handler(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh)
>   * Unregisters the file handler from the liveupdate core. This function
>   * reverses the operations of liveupdate_register_file_handler().
>   */
> -int liveupdate_unregister_file_handler(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh)
> +void liveupdate_unregister_file_handler(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh)
>  {
>  	if (!liveupdate_enabled())
> -		return -EOPNOTSUPP;
> +		return;
>  
>  	scoped_guard(rwsem_write, &luo_file_handler_lock) {
>  		luo_flb_unregister_all(fh);

Is there an O(N^2) complexity issue in luo_flb_unregister_all() due to
redundant list traversals?

In luo_flb_unregister_all(), list_for_each_entry_safe is used to iterate over
fh->flb_list, passing each flb to luo_flb_unregister_one().

Inside luo_flb_unregister_one(), it iterates over fh->flb_list from the
beginning again to find the link matching the flb in order to delete it.

Could passing the iter link directly to a deletion helper avoid the redundant
O(N) traversal per element?

>  		list_del(&ACCESS_PRIVATE(fh, list));
>  	}
> -
> -	return 0;
>  }

> diff --git a/kernel/liveupdate/luo_flb.c b/kernel/liveupdate/luo_flb.c
> index 5b61c0844a49..874830f8e44d 100644
> --- a/kernel/liveupdate/luo_flb.c
> +++ b/kernel/liveupdate/luo_flb.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -476,18 +476,16 @@ int liveupdate_register_flb(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh,
>   * owner module (acquired during registration) is released.
>   *
>   */
> -int liveupdate_unregister_flb(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh,
> -			      struct liveupdate_flb *flb)
> +void liveupdate_unregister_flb(struct liveupdate_file_handler *fh,
> +			       struct liveupdate_flb *flb)
>  {

Can missing module reference counting lead to a use-after-free during file
preservation and unregistration?

The comment above mentions releasing a module reference "acquired during
registration," but tracing the code reveals that try_module_get() and
module_put() are missing from both registration and unregistration paths.

In luo_preserve_file(), luo_file_handler_lock is acquired in read mode to
find a compatible file handler (fh), but the lock is released before fh
is used.

Concurrently, the module owning fh can be unloaded. Its exit function calls
liveupdate_unregister_file_handler(fh), which acquires the lock, unregisters
fh, and returns, allowing the module's memory to be freed.

luo_preserve_file() then calls luo_flb_file_preserve(fh) and
fh->ops->preserve(...) on the freed pointer, jumping to unmapped memory.

Furthermore, if a module unloads while its fh is referenced by preserved
files in a luo_file_set, a subsequent call to luo_file_unpreserve_files()
will dereference the freed fh->ops->unpreserve pointer.

Should a try_module_get(fh->ops->owner) be added inside the lock in
luo_preserve_file() (or during registration) to prevent module unload while
the handler is in use?

>  	if (!liveupdate_enabled())
> -		return -EOPNOTSUPP;
> +		return;
>  
>  	guard(rwsem_write)(&luo_flb_lock);
>  	guard(rwsem_write)(&ACCESS_PRIVATE(fh, flb_lock));
>  
>  	luo_flb_unregister_one(fh, flb);
> -
> -	return 0;
>  }
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Missing module reference counting leads to Use-After-Free during file preservation and unregistration.","severity":"Critical","severity_explanation":"1. In `luo_preserve_file()`, `luo_file_handler_lock` is acquired in read mode to find a compatible file handler (`fh`). Once found, the lock is released BEFORE `fh` is used.
2. Concurrently, the module owning `fh` can be unloaded. Its exit function calls `liveupdate_unregister_file_handler(fh)`, which acquires the lock, unregisters `fh`, and returns, allowing the module's memory to be freed.
3. `luo_preserve_file()` then calls `luo_flb_file_preserve(fh)` and `fh->ops->preserve(...)` on the freed pointer, jumping to unmapped memory and causing a kernel crash.
4. Furthermore, even if preservation completes safely, there is no active-user tracking. If a module unloads while its `fh` is referenced by preserved files in a `luo_file_set`, a subsequent call to `luo_file_unpreserve_files()` will dereference the freed `fh->ops->unpreserve` pointer.
5. The comment for `liveupdate_unregister_flb` explicitly mentions releasing a module reference "acquired during registration," but tracing the code reveals that `try_module_get()` and `module_put()` are completely missing from both registration and unregistration paths. A `try_module_get(fh->ops->owner)` is required inside the lock in `luo_preserve_file()` (or during registration) to prevent module unload while the handler is in use."},{"problem":"`WARN_ON` check in `liveupdate_register_flb` fails to catch zero-initialized handlers, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. In `liveupdate_register_flb`, the code checks `WARN_ON(list_empty(&ACCESS_PRIVATE(fh, list)))` to ensure the file handler has been registered.
2. If the caller erroneously passes a zero-initialized `fh` (e.g., a static struct that hasn't been passed to `liveupdate_register_file_handler`), `fh->private.list.next` is `NULL`.
3. The `list_empty` macro compares `head->next == head` (i.e., `NULL == &fh->private.list`), which evaluates to `false`. The `WARN_ON` check is therefore bypassed.
4. The function then proceeds to `list_add_tail(&link->list, &ACCESS_PRIVATE(fh, flb_list))`. Since `flb_list` is also zero-initialized, `head->prev` is `NULL`.
5. `list_add_tail` executes `head->prev->next = new`, which attempts to dereference `NULL`, causing a kernel panic. The check should explicitly verify initialization instead of using `list_empty`."},{"problem":"O(N^2) complexity in `luo_flb_unregister_all` due to redundant list traversals.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"1. In `luo_flb_unregister_all`, `list_for_each_entry_safe` is used to iterate over `fh->flb_list`, passing each `flb` to `luo_flb_unregister_one`.
2. Inside `luo_flb_unregister_one`, it again iterates over `fh->flb_list` from the beginning to find the link matching the `flb` in order to delete it.
3. While functionally safe (since `list_for_each_entry_safe` caches the next pointer), this results in an O(N^2) time complexity. Passing the `iter` link directly to a deletion helper would avoid the redundant O(N) traversal per element."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 5502094cb825f8d248d81d6f849c5b94a278d6c1
